# Phytoremediation

[English Brochure](/sites/default/files/phytoremediation_final_oct_2012.pdf "phytoremediation_final_oct_2012.pdf")

[Folleto en Español](/sites/default/files/phyto_spanish_august2021_final.pdf "phyto_spanish_august2021_final.pdf")

## Phytoremediation: using plants to treat environmental pollution

The term **phytoremediation** comes from the Ancient Greek word *phyto* meaning “plant” and the Latin word *remedium* meaning “restoring balance.” It is a technology that uses plants to treat environmental pollution problems. Plants are used either to remove or to stabilize (hold in place) pollution in the soil.

**The ultimate goal is to use plants to reduce the risk of human exposure.**

Compared to other methods used to remove toxic substances from soils, phytoremediation is often less expensive, although it may take a long time. Another benefit is that it covers the site with an attractive layer of plants that prevents wind and water from carrying the pollution to other places.

Two common strategies for phytoremediaton are: **Phytoextraction** and **Phytostabilization**

## Plant selection: critical for success

Picking the right plant for the job is important! Some factors to consider are:

**Plant Accumulation:** When plants take up water through their roots in polluted soils, they can also take up toxic substances. In this way, plants can accumulate (store) pollutants in roots, stems, and/or leaves. Some plants are very good at this, while other plants accumulate very little. Scientists can test plants and calculate an **“accumulation factor” (AF)** = (pollution taken up into plant) divided by (pollution in soil).

**Use of Native Plants:** There are several advantages to using native plants for **phytoremediation**:

**Variety of Plants:** Selection of a variety of plant types (grasses,  shrubs, and trees) helps to improve coverage of the area and develop a more robust ecosystem.

![Phytoextraction](/sites/default/files/styles/az_very_small/public/phytoextraction.jpg.webp?itok=-ddTAFuI)

## What is Phytoextraction?

The goal of phytoextraction is to use plants to **remove metal pollutants** from the soil.

![phytoextraction truck](/sites/default/files/styles/az_very_small/public/phytoextraction_truck.jpg.webp?itok=eqwUG1xo)

Phytoextraction is a good option if pollution is moving and spreading in the soil or water beneath the ground and the only way to stop it is to remove it. It is also a good option for smaller land areas, or if a polluted site is located on a valuable area of land.

Phytoextraction requires long-term maintenance and routine harvesting of the plants,  as well as safe disposal of polluted plant materials. Phytoextraction does not work for lead, but can be effective for arsenic, cadmium, and nickel.

![Phytostabilization](/sites/default/files/styles/az_very_small/public/phytostabilization.jpg.webp?itok=71p5CvHm)

## What is Phytostabilization?

Phytostabilization The goal of phytostabilization is to **stabilize metal or organic pollution** in the soil with a lasting cover of plants.

![phytostabilization multiple](/sites/default/files/styles/az_very_small/public/phytostabilization_multiple.jpg.webp?itok=Q3EiHw55)

Phytostabilization is a good option when pollution is not moving underground, but might be spreading in the air. It is also a good option for large land areas in which there is too much polluted soil to be removed. In addition, wildlife can safely eat the plants, due to low accumulation of pollutants.

Phytostabilization requires the growth of a healthy and strong layer of plants before human activity can resume on the land being treated.

## Types of Phytoremediation

The method of phytoremediation used depends on the type and amount of pollution, as well as what type of area it is found in.

**Establishing healthy soil:** When soil is highly polluted, it can be hard for any plants to grow. The site may require special preparation first, to establish healthy soil. Additions such as compost, manure, fertilizers, limestone, gypsum, and/or irrigation may be required for plants to grow.

## How long will cleanup take?

This depends on factors that are different at every site: what kind of pollution is present, and how much there is, which phytoremediation method is chosen, and what type of plants are used.

## Want to learn more about Phytoremediation?

For more information, please contact us at:

--- SOURCE 2: Phytoremediation: A Promising Approach for Revegetation of Heavy ... ---
URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full

SUMMARY:
There are advantages of using phytoremediation, which include: (i) economically feasible—phytoremediation is an autotrophic system powered by solar energy, therefore, simple to manage, and the cost of installation and maintenance is low, (ii) environment and eco-friendly—it can reduce exposure of the pollutants to the environment and ecosystem, (iii) applicability—it can be applied over a large-scale field and can easily be disposed, (iv) it prevents erosion and metal leaching through stabilizing heavy metals, reducing the risk of spreading of contaminants, (v) it can also improve soil fertility by releasing various organic matters to the soil (Aken et al., 2009; Wuana and Okieimen, 2011; Jacob et al., 2018).

Phytoremediation: A Promising Approach for Revegetation of Heavy Metal-Polluted Land
====================================================================================

[![Image 5: An Yan](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/949601/74)An Yan](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/949601)1[![Image 6: Yamin Wang](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/888176/74)Yamin Wang](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/888176)1![Image 7: Mohamed Lokman Mohd Yusof](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)Swee Ngin Tan 1![Image 8: Mohamed Lokman Mohd Yusof](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)Mohamed Lokman Mohd Yusof 2[![Image 9: Subhadip Ghosh,](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/909225/74)Subhadip Ghosh](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/909225)2,3[![Image 10: Zhong Chen,*](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/184462/74)Zhong Chen](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/184462)1,4*

*   1 Natural Sciences and Science Education, National Institute of Education, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore, Singapore

Heavy metal accumulation in soil has been rapidly increased due to various natural processes and anthropogenic (industrial) activities. As heavy metals are non-biodegradable, they persist in the environment, have potential to enter the food chain through crop plants, and eventually may accumulate in the human body through biomagnification. Owing to their toxic nature, heavy metal contamination has posed a serious threat to human health and the ecosystem. Therefore, remediation of land contamination is of paramount importance. Phytoremediation is an eco-friendly approach that could be a successful mitigation measure to revegetate heavy metal-polluted soil in a cost-effective way. To improve the efficiency of phytoremediation, a better understanding of the mechanisms underlying heavy metal accumulation and tolerance in plant is indispensable. In this review, we describe the mechanisms of how heavy metals are taken up, translocated, and detoxified in plants. We focus on the strategies applied to improve the efficiency of phytostabilization and phytoextraction, including the application of genetic engineering, microbe-assisted and chelate-assisted approaches.

Introduction
------------

With the development of industrialization and urbanization, the abundance of heavy metals in the environment has increased enormously during the past decades, which raised significant concerns throughout the world ([Suman et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B142); [Ashraf et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B7)). Heavy metals are a group of metallic chemical elements that have relatively high densities, atomic weights, and atomic numbers. The common heavy metals/metalloids include cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg), lead (Pb), arsenic (As), zinc (Zn), copper (Cu), nickel (Ni), and chromium (Cr). These heavy metals/metalloids originate from either natural or anthropogenic sources such as produced water generated in oil and gas industries ([Neff et al., 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B105); [Pichtel, 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B112)), use of phosphate fertilizers in agriculture ([Hamzah et al., 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B64); [Rafique and Tariq, 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B115)), sewage sludge ([Farahat and Linderholm, 2015](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B48)), metal mining and smelting ([Chen et al., 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B29)), pesticide application ([Iqbal et al., 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B73)), electroplating, and fossil fuel burning ([Muradoglu et al., 2015](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B104)).

Heavy metals are non-degradable by any biological or physical process and are persistent in the soil for a long period, which pose a long-term threat for the environment ([Suman et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B142)). According to their role in biological systems, heavy metals can be grouped as essential and non-essential. Essential heavy metals such as Cu, Fe, Mn, Ni, and Zn are required for physiological and biochemical processes during plant life cycle ([Cempel and Nikel, 2006](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B26)); however, they may become toxic when present in excess. Non-essential heavy metals like Pb, Cd, As, and Hg are highly toxic with no known function in plants ([Fasani et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B49)) and may cause environmental pollution and severely affect a variety of physiological and biochemical processes in crop plants and reduce agricultural productivity ([Clemens, 2006](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B31)). They can enter into the food chain through crops and accumulate in the human body through biomagnification, thus posing a great threat to human health ([Sarwar et al., 2010](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B132); [Rehman et al., 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B119)).

Hence, it is necessary to take remediation measures to prevent heavy metals from entering into terrestrial, atmospheric, and aquatic environments, and mitigate the contaminated land ([Gerhardt et al., 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B51); [Hasan et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B67)). So far, there are a variety of remediation approaches that have been developed to reclaim heavy metal-contaminated soil. These measures are mainly based on mechanical or physio-chemical techniques, such as soil incineration, excavation and landfill, soil washing, solidification, and electric field application ([Sheoran et al., 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B138); [Wuana and Okieimen, 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B164); [DalCorso et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B34)). However, there are limitations reported on these physicochemical approaches such as high cost, inefficiency when contaminants are present at low concentrations, irreversible changes to the physicochemical and biological properties of soils, which lead to the deterioration of the soil ecosystem, introduction of secondary pollutions ([Ali et al., 2013](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B2); [DalCorso et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B34)). Therefore, there is a need to develop cost-effective, efficient, and environment-friendly remediation technologies to reclaim heavy metal-contaminated soil.

Phytoremediation is a plant-based approach, which involves the use of plants to extract and remove elemental pollutants or lower their bioavailability in soil ([Berti and Cunningham, 2000](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B17)). Plants have the abilities to absorb ionic compounds in the soil even at low concentrations through their root system. Plants extend their root system into the soil matrix and establish rhizosphere ecosystem to accumulate heavy metals and modulate their bioavailability, thereby reclaiming the polluted soil and stabilizing soil fertility ([Ali et al., 2013](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B2); [Jacob et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B74); [DalCorso et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B34)). There are advantages of using phytoremediation, which include: (i) economically feasible—phytoremediation is an autotrophic system powered by solar energy, therefore, simple to manage, and the cost of installation and maintenance is low, (ii) environment and eco-friendly—it can reduce exposure of the pollutants to the environment and ecosystem, (iii) applicability—it can be applied over a large-scale field and can easily be disposed, (iv) it prevents erosion and metal leaching through stabilizing heavy metals, reducing the risk of spreading of contaminants, (v) it can also improve soil fertility by releasing various organic matters to the soil ([Aken et al., 2009](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B1); [Wuana and Okieimen, 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.00359/full#B164); [Jacob et al., 2018](https://ww

Plants have the abilities to absorb ionic compounds in the soil even at low concentrations through their root system. Plants extend their root system into the soil matrix and establish rhizosphere ecosystem to accumulate heavy metals and modulate their bioavailability, thereby reclaiming the polluted soil and stabilizing soil fertility ([Ali et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B2); [Jacob et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B74); [DalCorso et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B34)). There are advantages of using phytoremediation, which include: (i) economically feasible—phytoremediation is an autotrophic system powered by solar energy, therefore, simple to manage, and the cost of installation and maintenance is low, (ii) environment and eco-friendly—it can reduce exposure of the pollutants to the environment and ecosystem, (iii) applicability—it can be applied over a large-scale field and can easily be disposed, (iv) it prevents erosion and metal leaching through stabilizing heavy metals, reducing the risk of spreading of contaminants, (v) it can also improve soil fertility by releasing various organic matters to the soil ([Aken et al., 2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B1); [Wuana and Okieimen, 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B164); [Jacob et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B74)). During the past decades, numerous studies have been conducted to understand the molecular mechanisms underlying heavy metal tolerance and to develop techniques to improve phytoremediation efficiency. In the current review, the mechanisms of how heavy metals are taken up and translocated in plants are described, and the detoxification strategies (avoidance and tolerance) adopted by plants in response to heavy metal have been discussed. The main objective is to overview the recent advances in developing phytoremediation techniques, including the strategies to improve heavy metal bioavailability, tolerance, and accumulation. This review also highlights the application of genetic engineering to improve plant performance during phytoremediation.

Uptake and Translocation of Heavy Metals in Plants
--------------------------------------------------

There are series of processes involved in accumulation of heavy metal in plants, including heavy metal mobilization, root uptake, xylem loading, root-to-shoot transport, cellular compartmentation, and sequestration. Heavy metal mostly exists as insoluble form in soil, which is not bioavailable to plants. Plants can increase their bioavailability by releasing a variety of root exudates, which can change rhizosphere pH and increase heavy metal solubility ([Dalvi and Bhalerao, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B35)). The bioavailable metal is sorbed at the root surface and moves across the cellular membrane into the root cells. The uptake of heavy metals into roots occurs mainly through two pathways, apoplastic pathway (passive diffusion) and symplastic pathway (active transport against electrochemical potential gradients and concentration across the plasma membrane). The common uptake of heavy metals via symplastic pathway is an energy-dependent process mediated by metal ion carriers or complexing agents ([Peer et al., 2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B108)).

After entering into root cells, heavy metal ions can form complexes with various chelators, such as organic acids. These formed complexes including carbonate, sulfate, and phosphate precipitate, are then immobilized in the extracellular space (apoplastic cellular walls) or intracellular spaces (symplastic compartments, such as vacuoles) ([Ali et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B2)). The metal ions sequestered inside the vacuoles may transport into the stele and enter into the xylem stream via the root symplasm ([Thakur et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B147)) and subsequently are translocated to the shoots through xylem vessels. Through apoplast or symplast, they are transported and distributed in leaves, where the ions are sequestered in extracellular compartments (cell walls) or plant vacuole, thereby preventing accumulation of free metal ions in cytosol ([Tong et al., 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B152)).

### Heavy Metal Ion Transporter

Uptake and translocation of heavy metal in plant is mediated by a variety of molecules, including metal ion transporters and complexing agents. These specialized transporters (channel proteins) or H+-coupled carrier proteins are located in the plasma membrane of the root cell and are essential for the uptake of heavy metal ions from soil. They can transport specific metals across cellular membranes and mediate influx–efflux of metal translocation from roots to shoots ([DalCorso et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B34)). According to sequence homology, metal transporters identified, so far, have been classified into several families, such as ZIP, HMAs, MTPs, and NRAMPs.

Transporters of the ZIP family (ZRT–IRT-like proteins) are involved in heavy metal accumulation processes including uptake and transport of many cations (e.g., Fe, Mn, and Zn) from root to shoot ([Guerinot, 2000](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B57)). For example, Zn hyperaccumulator _Thlaspi caerulescens_ and _Arabidopsis halleri_ roots have enhanced Zn uptake in comparison to non-hyperaccumulator species, which is correlated with enhanced expression of some ZIP family members in hyperaccumulator ([Assunção et al., 2001](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B8)). The P1B-type ATPases of heavy metal transporting ATPases (HMAs) transporter family are involved in the transport of heavy metals (such as Zn, Cd, Co, and Pb) and play a vital role in metal homeostasis and tolerance ([Axelsen and Palmgren, 2001](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B9); [Williams and Mills, 2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B161)). HMA3, a vacuolar P1B-ATPase, is involved in compartmentation of Zn, Cd, Co, and Pb by regulating their sequestration into the vacuole ([Williams and Mills, 2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B161); [Hanikenne and Baurain, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B65)). Another transporter of the family, HMA4, is involved in long-distance root-to-shoot translocation of Zn and Cd ([Verret et al., 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B157)). Overexpression of HMA4 enhanced Cd and Zn efflux from the root symplasm into the xylem vessels and promoted metal tolerance. Another group of transporters that tightly regulate metal homeostasis is metal transporter proteins (MTPs) family, which is involved in the translocation of metals (such as Zn and Ni) toward internal compartments and extracellular space ([Gustin et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B62)). MTP1, a vacuolar Zn 2+/H+ antiporter, which localized at both vacuolar and plasma membrane, is involved in Zn accumulation as well as Zn tolerance ([Desbrosses-Fonrouge et al., 2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B39)). MTP members are also involved in Ni vacuolar storage in _Thlaspi goesingense_ ([Persans et al., 2001](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B111)). The naturally resistant associated macrophage proteins (NRAMPs) are also involved in the transport of many heavy metal ions including Cu 2+, Mn 2+, Co 2+, Fe 2+, and Cd 2+ ([Supek et al., 1997](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B144); [Cailliatte et al., 2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B25); [Bastow et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B14)). AtNRAMP1 is localized in the plasma membrane and mediates Fe and Mn transport ([Cailliatte et al., 2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B25)). NRAMP3 and NRAMP4 are localized in the tonoplast and mediate the export of stored Fe from the vacuole in germinating seed ([Bastow et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B14)).

Besides metal ion transporter, complexing agents including organic acids and amino acids act as metal ligands to mediate chelation of heavy metal ions. For example, citrate is a major chelator for Fe and Ni in the xylem ([Tiffin, 1970](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B149); [Lee et al., 1977](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B87)), while Ni may also be chelated by histidine ([Krämer et al., 1996](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B81)).

Detoxification Mechanism
------------------------

Heavy metal detoxification is a key prerequisite for the implementation of phytoremediation ([Thakur et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B147)). Generally, there are two defense strategies adopted by plants to cope with the toxicity of heavy metals: avoidance and tolerance. By these two mechanisms, plants manage to maintain the cellular concentrations of heavy metals below the toxicity threshold levels ([Hall, 2002](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B63)).

### Avoidance

Avoidance strategy refers to the ability of plants to limit the uptake of heavy metals and restrict their movement into plant tissues through root cells ([Dalvi and Bhalerao, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B35)). It works as the first line of defense at extracellular level through a range of mechanisms such as root sorption, metal ion precipitation, and metal exclusion ([Dalvi and Bhalerao, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B35)). Upon exposure to heavy metals, plants first try to immobilize them either through root sorption or by modifying metal ions. A variety of root exudates, such as organic acids and amino acids, act as a heavy metal ligand to form stable heavy metal complexes in the rhizosphere ([Dalvi and Bhalerao, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B35)). Some root exudates can change the pH of rhizosphere, which lead to precipitation of heavy metals, thereby limiting their bioavailability and lessening the toxicity ([Dalvi and Bhalerao, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B35)). Through metal exclusion mechanism, exclusion barriers exist between the root system and the shoot system to limit the access of heavy metals from soil only to roots; the uptake and root-to-shoot transport is restricted to protect aerial parts against harmful heavy metals. Moreover, arbuscular mycorrhizas can restrict the entry of heavy metals into the root by absorption, adsorption, or chelation of heavy metals in the rhizosphere, thus, working as an exclusion barrier for heavy metal uptake ([Hall, 2002](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B63)). Embedding the heavy metals in the plant cell walls is another mechanism of heavy metal avoidance ([Memon and Schröder, 2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B100)). Cell wall pectins consist of carboxylic groups of polygalacturonic acids, which are negatively charged and able to bind heavy metals. Therefore, cell wall acts as a cation exchanger to restrict entry of free heavy metal ions into the cells ([Ernst et al., 1992](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B47)).

### Tolerance

Once the heavy metal ions get entry into the cytosol, tolerance strategy is adopted by the plants to cope with the toxicity of accumulated metal ions. It is the second line of defense at intracellular level through various mechanisms such as inactivation, chelation, and compartmentalization of heavy metal ions ([Dalvi and Bhalerao, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B35)).

When excess heavy metal ions are accumulated inside the cytosol, plants have to detoxify them in order to minimize their toxic effects ([Manara, 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B95)). This is mainly achieved through chelation by complexation of heavy metal ions with ligands. Through chelation, the concentrations of free metal ions are reduced to relatively low levels. There are many organic and inorganic ligands in the cytoplasm that mediate heavy metal chelation. The organic compounds involved in heavy metal ion chelation include organic acids, amino acids, phytochelatins (PCs), metallothioneins (MTs), and cell wall proteins/pectins/polyphenols ([Hall, 2002](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B63); [Sharma and Dietz, 2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B136); [Gupta et al., 2013b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B61)). Organic acids within cells prevent the persistence of heavy metals as free ions in the cytoplasm by complexing and reducing their bioavailability to plants. For example, citrate mediates the chelation of Ni in _T. goesingense_ leaves ([Krämer et al., 2000](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B82)), while acetic and citric acids bind Cd in leaves of _Solanum nigrum_ ([Sun et al., 2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B143)). In addition, malate is involved in chelation of Zn in _A. halleri_ ([Sarret et al., 2002](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B130)). Heavy metal stress induces the accumulation of certain kinds of amino acid. For example, Cd can induce the production of cysteine in _Arabidopsis thaliana_ ([Domínguez−Solís et al., 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B41)), while Ni hyperaccumulation induces histidine accumulation ([Harper et al., 1999](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B66)). High accumulation of proline is also induced by Cd, Pb, Zn, and Cu stress ([Roy and Bera, 2002](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B126)). These amino acids can detoxify heavy metals by chelating heavy metal ions within cells and xylem sap ([Rai, 2002](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B117)). PCs and MTs are also induced in response to high levels of heavy metals. For example, Cd is chelated by PCs in tobacco leaves ([Vögeli-Lange and Wagner, 1990](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B158)), while MTs mediate the response to Cu stress in _Silene vulgaris_, as increased expression of MT gene is associated with enhanced Cu tolerance ([van Hoof et al., 2001](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B155)).

After chelation, the complexes of ligands with heavy metals are actively transported from the cytosol into inactive compartments, such as vacuole where the complexes are stored without toxicity ([Tong et al., 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B152)). Sequestration and vacuolar compartmentalization provide an effective protection against the detrimental effects of heavy metals by removing toxic heavy metal ions from sensitive sites of the cell where cell division and respiration occur, thereby reducing the interactions between heavy metal ions and cellular metabolic processes and avoiding damages to cell functions ([Sheoran et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B138)). The uptake, translocation, and detoxification of heavy metals in plants are illustrated in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#F1).

Besides vacuoles, heavy metal ions can be sequestrated and compartmentalized into other locations, such as leaf petioles, leaf sheathes, and trichomes ([Robinson et al., 2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B124); [Eapen and D’souza, 2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B43)), where heavy metals cause less damage to the plant. Heavy metals can also be translocated to old leaves and removed from the plant body by natural leaf shedding ([Thakur et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B147)). For example, Zn is moved to _Plantago lanceolata_ leaves just during the last week prior to leaf shedding and eventually removed from the plant after leaf fall ([Ernst et al., 1992](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7203417/#B47)).

Highlights
----------

*   •[Phytoremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/phytoremediation "Learn more about Phytoremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is an economical and sustainable technology for environmental cleanup. 
*   •Plant species known to utilize different phyto-mechanisms during phytoremediation of diverse pollutants has been presented. 
*   •Different aids to overcome the limitations of phytoremediation are described. 
*   •Strengths and limitations inherent with the phytoremediation technique is supported by recent research. 

Abstract
--------

With current intensive agriculture practices and industrialization, pollution of natural resources like land and water with heavy metals, organic pollutants, radionuclides, pesticides, and fertilizers has become a major concern. Phytoremediation is a cost-effective and environmentally friendly technique that utilizes plants to immobilize, uptake, reduce toxicity, stabilize, or degrade the compounds that are released into the environment from different sources. Studies have shown that heavy metals, organic contaminants, radionuclides, antibiotics, and pesticides can be remediated using plants. Though phytoremediation has been practiced since decades, it is still an emerging technology. This review article summarizes existing information and synthesizes the recent findings on plant species suitable for use in phytoremediation through utilizing different mechanisms, aids that can enhance the efficiency of phytoremediation processes, and strengths and limitations that comes with the application of this technique. Diverse plants remediate different pollutants at different rates through one or multiple mechanisms. The limitations of phytoremediation can be overcome by using several aids including natural and chemical amendments, genetic engineering and natural microbial stimulation. Given the low-cost of phytoremediation compared to conventional technology and sustainability associated with plants and use of renewable energy, phytoremediation can be a reliable solution for a sustainable and economical remediation of soil and water from the organic and inorganic pollutants.

1. Introduction
---------------

Rapid rise in global population accompanied by ensuing demands for food, water, energy, timber, and other ecosystem services has led to severe stresses on the [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/natural-resources "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the environment ([Kamran et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0141); [Saleem et al., 2020c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0235); [Zaheer et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0298)). [Anthropogenic activities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/anthropogenic-activity "Learn more about Anthropogenic activities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") including the agricultural intensification practices ([Mózner et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0192)), speedy urbanization, and rapid industrialization ([Cherniwchan,2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0049); [Wu et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0289)) are causing serious environmental pollution by [heavy metals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/heavy-metal "Learn more about heavy metals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HMs) and metalloids ([Anyakora et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0015); [He et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0118); [Su,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0261)), radionuclides ([Hu et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0125); [Prakash et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0215)), organic compounds ([Afzal et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0003)), agrochemicals ([Malik et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0177); [Sunitha et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0265)), and oil spills ([Ron & Rosenberg,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0228)). Mining activities, disposal of effluents from industries and [residential areas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/residential-area "Learn more about residential areas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), heavy use of fertilizers and pesticides, and irrigation of contaminated water have led to contamination of soils ([Awad et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0020); [Tang et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0267); [Teng et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0270)). Mining leads to disturbance of many soil properties, including pH, electrical conductivity, and cation exchange capacity ([Saleem et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0233)). Consequently, HMs such as Copper (Cu), Lead (Pb), Zinc (Zn), Nickel (Ni), Cadmium (Cd), Chromium (Cr), Iron (Fe), Manganese (Mn) and organic pollutants such as hydrocarbons, aromatic compounds, phenols, organo-chlorinated compounds, pesticides have been found at elevated concentrations in the soil and sediments ([Gutiérrez-Ginés et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0111); [Liu et al., 2017b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0169); [Song et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0254)).

HMs accumulation in soil affects [soil microbial population](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-microbial-populations "Learn more about soil microbial population from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), processes and their activity ([Huang et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0126); [Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)). The effects of HMs on microbial population changes and enzymatic activities are dependent upon the type of HMs and their chemical affinity ([Karaca et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0142)). For examples, cadmium (Cd) affects [ureases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/urease "Learn more about ureases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), proteases and basic phosphatases ([Lorenz et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0170)); lead (Pb) affects [catalases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/catalase "Learn more about catalases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ureases, [invertase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/invertase "Learn more about invertase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [acid phosphatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/acid-phosphatase "Learn more about acid phosphatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Belyaeva et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0032)); Arsenic (As) affects [sulfatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/sulfatase "Learn more about sulfatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and phosphatase ([Speir et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0258)); and copper (Cu) restricts b-glucosidase ([Geiger et al., 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0097)). High chromium (VI) concentration in soil exhibits distinct toxicity characteristics causing negative effects in soil microbial cell metabolism ([Huang et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0126)).

The [ingestion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ingestion "Learn more about ingestion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of food crops contaminated with HMs can lead to [bioaccumulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioaccumulation "Learn more about bioaccumulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of HMs and subsequent biomagnification along the higher [trophic levels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/trophic-level "Learn more about trophic levels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the food chain can threaten both animal and human health ([Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)). HMs accumulation in human body can cause serious complications in human health such as Itai-itai disease due to chronic Cd exposure ([Pan et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0205)); kidney dysfunction and anemia due to Zn ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)); [mucosa](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mucous-membrane "Learn more about mucosa from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") corrosion, hepatic system failure and [central nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/central-nervous-system "Learn more about central nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") damage due to Cu ([Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)); [skin irritation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/skin-irritation "Learn more about skin irritation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nervous-system "Learn more about nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") complications due to Ni ([Argun et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0016)); cardiovascular problems and damage to nervous system due to Pb ([Kazemipour et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0144); [Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)); abortion, nervous breakdown and skin rashes due to Hg ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)); cancer due to Cr ([Zohdi et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0304)); and carcinogenic and [immune system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/immune-system "Learn more about immune system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") dysfunction due to As ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)).

Among different organic contaminants, chlorinated solvents, petroleum hydrocarbons explosives ([Alkorta & Garbisu,2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0011)), polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), pyrene, and trinitrotoluene (TNT) ([Chekol & Vough,2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0045)) are major soil contaminants. Pesticides that often contaminate soils include [atrazine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/atrazine "Learn more about atrazine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [dieldrin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/dieldrin "Learn more about dieldrin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [hexachlorobenzene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/hexachlorobenzene "Learn more about hexachlorobenzene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), dichlorodiphenyltrichlorethane (DDT), and 1,2,4- trichlorobenzene ([Chiou et al., 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0050)). These pollutants seriously affect the [soil biota](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-biota "Learn more about soil biota from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), mineral nutrients availability, and soil productivity because of their persistence in soil for long period of time and toxicity.

Like soils, [water resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/water-resources "Learn more about water resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have also been contaminated by different pollutants. Urbanization and industrialization activities release toxic heavy metals through the sewage, runoff and effluents contributing to largest share of factors for water contamination ([Masindi & Muedi,2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0182); [Parveen et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0206); [Saleem et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0233)). Over 80% of world's wastewater reach to environment without treatment causing nearly 58% of health issues like diarrhea, which is a major cause for early child dealth ([Connor et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0054)). Among metals, Cu, Ni, Pb, Cd, Cr, U, and Zn frequently contaminate water resources ([Prasad & Freitas,2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0216)). The HMs degrade the quality of water and can lead to water-borne health hazards through heavy metal accumulation in the food chain ([Wongsasuluk et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0287)). Apart from HMs, organic pollutants contaminate water resources either by erosion, seepage, sludge and effluents from municipal waste and industries, and runoff from agricultural practices ([Diez,2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0064)) and mining ([Tanhan et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0268)). These contaminants can accumulate over time and negatively affect the aquatic as well as [terrestrial ecosystems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/terrestrial-ecosystem "Learn more about terrestrial ecosystems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Remediation of the soil and water pollutants for sustaining ecosystem processes and functions is one of the major challenges our society [faces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/face "Learn more about faces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") today. Many physical, chemical, and biological approaches have been employed for remediating the environmental pollution, however, their applications are limited due to the cost and labor requirements, safety hazards, and risks to ecosystems ([Ali et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0010)). An alternative technique, [phytoremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/phytoremediation "Learn more about phytoremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), can be an effective technique and is gaining popularity, acceptance, and implementation.

Phytoremediation technique utilizes plants to remediate the contaminated media including soil and water. Phytoremediation is an economically and environmentally favorable technique as it utilizes [green plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/viridiplantae "Learn more about green plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to contain, sequester or detoxify contaminants from contaminated soil and water ([Ashraf et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0019)). Phytoremediation utilizes many mechanisms including degradation (rhizo-degradation, phytodegradation), accumulation (phytoextraction, rhizofiltration), dissipation (phytovolatilization), and immobilization (hydraulic control and phytostabilization) to degrade, remove, or immobilize the pollutants ([Pivetz,2001b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0214)). Depending upon the contaminants, plants utilize one or more of these mechanisms to reduce their concentrations from soil and water. For examples, plants uptake and accumulate the HMs in their tissues ([Mahar et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0176)) and degrade the organic pollutants ([Saleem et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0234)) reducing their toxicity from soil and water resources.

Different plant uses different methods or their combinations to remediate soil and water depending upon the contaminant types, forms, and media. Contaminated ground water can be remediated through phytodegradation, phytovolatilization, rhizo-filtration, rhizo-degradation and phytodegradation. Surface and wastewater contaminations can be treated by [rhizofiltration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rhizofiltration "Learn more about rhizofiltration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), phytodegradation or rhizodegradation. Soil, sediments or sludges contaminations are remediated through phytoextraction, phytodegradations, phytostabilization, rhizodegradation or phytovolatilization.

Ideal plant species for phytoremediation purpose should be hardy in nature, high biomass-producing, tolerant to toxic effects of metals and contaminants, easy to cultivate, have high absorption capacity, and be non-attractive for herbivory ([Adesodun et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0002); [Sakakibara et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0231); [Shabani & Sayadi,2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0243)). In many cases, plant species cannot perform well and need aids to enhance phytoremediation. Such aids include soil amendments like biochar ([Paz-Ferreiro et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0207)), Ethylene Diamine Tetraacetic Acid (EDTA) ([Shahid et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0244)), endophytic bacteria ([Afzal et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0003)), [Arbuscular mycorrhiza](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/arbuscular-mycorrhiza "Learn more about Arbuscular mycorrhiza from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Gaur & Adholeya,2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0095)) or even [transgenic plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/transgenic-plant "Learn more about transgenic plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Abhilash et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0001); [Kawahigashi,2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0143)). They promote the phytoremediation process either by reducing the toxicity of pollutants or increasing the availability of pollutants or promoting the growth of plants.

Previous articles have provided information regarding techniques ([Jadia & Fulekar,2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0132); [Sharma & Pathak,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0245); [Willey,2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0285)), plants used ([Banuelos et al., 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0027); [Cheng,2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0047); [Pilon-Smits & Pilon,2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0211)), factors affecting the processes ([Antoniadis et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0014); [Sheoran et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0246)), aids or stimulators ([Coninx et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0053); [Hovsepyan & Greipsson,2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0123); [Ullah et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0273)) and are either specific to soil ([Belouchrani et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0031); [Romeh,2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0227); [Waigi et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0278)) or water resources ([Nazir et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0197); [Rezania et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0226); [Ugya & Imam,2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0272)). enriched_points.expanded_content: Phytoremediation is often more affordable than traditional remediation methods, utilizing solar energy for plant growth, reducing operational costs for installation and maintenance.
This green technology reduces pollutant mobility, enhances air quality, and utilizes plants to stabilize contaminants, thereby protecting ecosystems from environmental degradation.
Phytoremediation promotes diverse plant growth, leading to improved habitat quality and ecological balance, which supports various species in contaminated areas.
Plants contribute organic matter, enhancing soil structure and fertility, while also promoting microbial activity, which is essential for a thriving ecosystem.
